title: Changing column type without downtime (varchar to text)
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add new column bio_text TEXT
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. ALTER TYPE varchar(N) to text in PG is metadata-only but can be slow on bloated tables.
2. Add-new column safer for very large tables.
3. App-level length validation moves to schema layer.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct ALTER TYPE on huge table locks for minutes.

title: jQuery $.ajax → fetch
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Wrapper handles error semantics + AbortController.
2. Caller code unchanged across switch.
3. Eventually drop wrapper if fetch sufficient.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct sed: error semantics differ; promise rejection vs callback err.

title: Saga pattern for distributed transactions

current_state.description: Single DB transaction across services impossible
current_state.code_sample: // commit order + decrement inventory + charge — all-or-nothing across services

target_state.code_sample: start saga → step 1 → step 2 → step 3; on failure, run compensations in reverse

migration_strategy: Phase 2: Define steps + compensations per step
Phase 4: Persist saga state for restart
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Saga = sequence of local transactions; if one fails, run compensations.
2. Orchestrator (central) or choreography (event-driven).
3. Idempotent steps so retries safe.
4. State persisted to survive crashes.
5. Test: every step's failure tested.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Partial commits leave inconsistent state.

title: Webpack 4 → Vite migration
migration_strategy: Phase 3: New Vite config, both build paths
Phase 4: Dev uses Vite; prod still Webpack until parity
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Some Webpack plugins don't have Vite equivalents — fork or rewrite.
2. Process.env handling differs (import.meta.env in Vite).
3. Test prod bundle parity (size, behavior).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct translation: process.env undefined, plugins missing.

title: Audit log retention migration (cold storage)

current_state.description: audit_log table 2TB; queries slow; backups massive

target_state.description: Hot last 90 days in DB; older in S3 Parquet

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Archive script: dump old rows to Parquet to S3
Phase 3: DELETE archived rows in chunks
Phase 5: Document query path (hot DB + Athena for cold)
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Cold storage cheap, slow.
2. Athena/BigQuery for ad-hoc queries.
3. Keep recent (90 days) hot for common queries.
4. Recurring job maintains tier.
what_breaks_naive_approach: DELETE without archive: compliance violation.

title: Adding versioned events without breaking subscribers
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Publish both v1 + v2 events
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Topic per version OR schemaVersion field in payload.
2. Track subscriber acks per version.
3. Schema registry enforces.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Modify in place: all subscribers fail at once.

title: Replacing custom CSS framework with Tailwind

current_state.description: Custom CSS framework with 5000 utility classes

migration_strategy: Phase 2: Map custom classes to Tailwind equivalents
Phase 4: Lint rule warns on legacy class use
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Custom → Tailwind mapping table.
2. Per-component cleanup.
3. Linter prevents new legacy.
4. Tailwind purge removes unused.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Full rewrite: visual regressions across hundreds of pages.

title: Migrating from int IDs to UUIDs

current_state.description: Sequential int IDs; enumeration security risk

migration_strategy: Phase 2: Populate uuid for existing rows
Phase 3: App accepts both via lookup
Phase 5: Internal joins migrate to uuid
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Type change in place impossible at scale.
2. Both coexist; app routes ID type via length/format.
3. URL exposure: only uuid in public URLs.
4. FKs: maintain both until joins migrate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ALTER TYPE: locks for hours; all clients fail.

title: Introduce dependency injection without IoC framework

target_state.description: DI via constructor or function args

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking in tests instead of injecting
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add optional param with default to current singleton
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. No need for nestjs-style IoC for most cases.
2. Composition root (entrypoint) wires dependencies.
3. Each function declares its deps explicitly.
4. Pure functions remain pure.
what_breaks_naive_approach: IoC framework: heavy, complex; team learns new abstractions.

title: PG version upgrade 14 → 16 with logical replication
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Test PG 16 in staging; all extensions work
Phase 2: Set up logical replication from 14 to 16
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Logical replication keeps both in sync.
2. Cutover = brief read-only window.
3. Test extensions (pg_stat_statements, pgcrypto, etc.) on 16.
rollback_procedure: Switch app back to 14 (still receiving via replication)
solution:
-- On PG14 (source)
ALTER SYSTEM SET wal_level = 'logical';
-- restart
CREATE PUBLICATION app_pub FOR ALL TABLES;

-- On PG16 (target)
CREATE SUBSCRIPTION app_sub CONNECTION 'host=pg14 dbname=app user=replicator password=...' PUBLICATION app_pub;

-- monitor lag
SELECT pg_replication_lag(); -- on subscriber

-- cutover:
-- 1. Set app to read-only
-- 2. Wait for lag = 0
-- 3. Switch app to PG16
-- 4. Sync sequences manually
SELECT setval('orders_id_seq', (SELECT MAX(id) FROM orders));
what_breaks_naive_approach: pg_upgrade in maintenance: hours of downtime.

title: Moment.js → date-fns
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Format string differences: 'YYYY' (moment) vs 'yyyy' (date-fns).
2. Tests guard against drift.
3. Per-file = small PRs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Format string mistakes: dates rendered wrong.

title: Background worker extraction

current_state.description: Web server handles long-running tasks (email sends, report generation)
current_state.code_sample: app.post('/report', async (req, res) => { await generateReport(); res.json(...); })
current_state.business_drivers: Web requests time out; uneven CPU load

target_state.benefits: Web requests fast; workers scale independently

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Worker = same code path with timer
migration_strategy: Phase 2: Web enqueues; legacy path stays as fallback
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Worker is separate process, separate deploy.
2. Retry with backoff; DLQ for permanent failures.
3. Idempotent jobs.
4. Observability: queue depth, worker count.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Long-running web requests: ALB timeouts; reverse-proxy disconnects.

title: Migration to event sourcing for a subdomain

current_state.description: Current state stored only; history lost

target_state.code_sample: events table (append-only); projections rebuild current state
target_state.benefits: Full history; can rebuild any view

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mixing CRUD + event sourcing without clarity
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Events table; dual-write (CRUD + emit event)
Phase 2: Projection from events matches CRUD
Phase 4: CRUD removed; events as truth
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Event sourcing is heavy; only worth it where history matters.
2. Snapshots speed up replay.
3. Projections async; tolerate lag.
4. Schema evolution: event upcasters.
rollback_procedure: Read from CRUD if projection broken
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throw out CRUD: no fallback, single point of failure.

title: Hybrid CMS migration
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Headless CMS deployed; Next.js builds against it
Phase 2: New pages on Next; WP for legacy
Phase 3: Migrate content type by type
Phase 4: Redirects from WP URLs to new
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. URL stability for SEO.
2. Content migration scripted (WP API → CMS API).
3. Run both; reverse proxy decides.
what_breaks_naive_approach: URL changes: SEO collapses.

title: Adding stored procedure without locks

current_state.description: Need atomic credit transfer; currently 2 statements in app

migration_strategy: Phase 3: Deploy app using function alongside fallback
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. CREATE FUNCTION is cheap.
2. Drop+recreate to update (test downtime).
3. Treat as code: version-controlled migrations.
what_breaks_naive_approach: App-level transfer with non-locking SELECT: double-spend.

title: Migrating from REST polling to WebSockets
migration_strategy: Phase 1: WS endpoint deployed alongside polling
Phase 2: Clients prefer WS, fall back to polling on failure
Phase 3: Polling rate reduced when WS connected
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. WS preferred; polling fallback for unreliable networks.
2. Auth: same session token over WS handshake.
3. Heartbeat; reconnect with backoff.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Remove polling first: clients on bad networks lose connection.

title: Tailwind v3 → v4 (engine change)
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. v4 engine ≠ v3; behavior subtly different.
2. Visual regression suite essential.
3. Plugin compatibility check.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Custom plugins may not work; visual drift.

title: Migrating from CITEXT to LOWER(text) for case-insensitive

target_state.description: email TEXT + UNIQUE INDEX on LOWER(email)

anti_patterns_to_avoid: ALTER TYPE without considering existing data
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add functional index on LOWER(email) CONCURRENTLY
Phase 4: Drop CITEXT extension if unused
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Functional index allows case-insensitive lookup without CITEXT.
2. App must lowercase consistently.
3. CITEXT extension can be removed once nothing uses it.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ALTER TYPE on busy table: locks.

title: Cross-region data migration

current_state.description: All data in us-east-1; need EU presence

migration_strategy: Phase 2: New EU users land in EU
Phase 3: Existing EU users migrated batch
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Per-user region locked at signup.
2. Migration: identify EU users, dump, restore in EU, redirect.
3. App routes by user region.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cross-region reads: latency spike; GDPR risk.

title: Replacing global state with explicit threading
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Globals = hidden coupling.
2. Explicit args = visible.
3. Composition root assembles.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Global mutation between tests: false passes/failures.

title: Microfrontend introduction (Module Federation)

current_state.description: Monolithic React app; multiple teams stepping on each other

migration_strategy: Phase 3: Shared deps via shared config
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Webpack 5/Vite plugin federation.
2. Shared React version mandatory.
3. Version pinning for stability.
4. Caching strategy for remotes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: MF without contracts: React duplication, version mismatches.

title: Switching from Mongoose to plain MongoDB driver
migration_strategy: Phase 3: Validation via Zod at boundary
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Native client thinner.
2. Validation handled explicitly.
3. Per-collection migration.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Drop validation: bad data ships.

title: Server-side enums → client-side derived

current_state.business_drivers: Drift between client and server enums

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Contracts package with shared enums
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Single source = no drift.
2. Package versioned; consumers update.
3. Pact-like contract testing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Drift: client sends bad enum; server rejects.

title: GraphQL schema deprecation
migration_strategy: Phase 2: Per-client field usage tracking via Apollo Studio or custom
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. GraphQL deprecation discoverable in introspection.
2. Field-level telemetry: which clients still ask.
3. Per-client outreach with proof of usage.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Remove without checking: clients break.

title: Replacing axios with native fetch
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Wrapper providing axios-like API over fetch
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Axios has interceptors, retries, transformers. Wrapper provides minimal needed subset.
2. AbortController for cancellation.
3. Test thoroughly — fetch behavior differs (4xx doesn't throw).
what_breaks_naive_approach: fetch doesn't throw 4xx; many code paths assume axios throws.

title: Migrating from blob storage to chunked

current_state.description: Large files stored as bytea in Postgres

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Files written to S3 going forward
Phase 2: Backfill existing files to S3
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. New writes immediately go to S3.
2. Backfill in chunks.
3. Reads with fallback during transition.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single ALTER + bulk MOVE: DB OOM.

title: Extracting search to dedicated service

current_state.description: Search done via SQL LIKE in monolith

migration_strategy: Phase 3: Indexer service consumes change events
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. CDC from DB → ES.
2. Backfill: bulk index existing.
3. Eventual consistency acceptable for search.
4. Reindex pipeline for schema changes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: LIKE search: full table scan; slow + uncached.

title: Recomputing derived columns at scale

current_state.description: orders.total_cents wrong for some rows due to past bug

reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Backup so revertable.
2. Recompute from source data.
3. Audit who was changed.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single UPDATE: locks.

title: Migrating off a deprecated payment processor
migration_strategy: Phase 3: Existing customer cards moved via Stripe's transfer API
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Card data: use Stripe's compliant card migration API (no PCI burden).
2. New customers Stripe-only.
3. Reconciliation: every charge tracked in both systems during transition.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Card data loss; PCI risk.

title: Switching primary key strategy mid-product

current_state.description: int PK; want ULID for distributed-friendliness

reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. PK type change near-impossible at scale.
2. New column + dual-PK period.
3. Gradual FK migration.
4. ULID benefits: time-sortable + UUID-strong randomness.
what_breaks_naive_approach: PK type change in place: impossible.
